name: Myanmar (Republic of the Union of Myanmar / ပြည်ထောင်စုသမ္မတ မြန်မာနိုင်ငံ)
summary: Myanmar is a Southeast Asian mixed-legal-system state combining English common-law substantive heritage (via colonial inheritance) with religious-community personal laws — Buddhist law (for Burmese Buddhists, via the Burma Buddhist Women's Special Marriage Act 1954 and customary Dhammathat tradition), Hindu law, Christian law, and Islamic law. Family law for Burmese Buddhists operates under customary Dhammathat with statutory supplementation; Muslim personal status operates under the Burma Muslim Dissolution of Marriages Act 1953. The Supreme Court of the Union (ပြည်ထောင်စုတရားလွှတ်တော်ချုပ်) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Constitutional Tribunal operates separate constitutional review. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in District Courts. Psychology profession is regulated through the Ministry of Health framework. Myanmar is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the welfare-of-the-child standard. Myanmar is non-Hague Convention.

statutory_framework.citation: Burma Buddhist Women's Special Marriage Act 1954
statutory_framework.title: Buddhist Women's Special Marriage Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute governing Burmese Buddhist marriage and family matters in conjunction with customary Dhammathat tradition.

statutory_framework.citation: Burma Muslim Dissolution of Marriages Act 1953
statutory_framework.title: Muslim Dissolution of Marriages Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute governing Muslim divorce matters.

statutory_framework.title: Guardians and Wards Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Colonial-era federal statute on guardianship — applies across religious communities residually.

statutory_framework.title: Child Rights Law
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal children's-rights statute aligned with UNCRC; replaced 1993 Child Law.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of the Union (ပြည်ထောင်စုတရားလွှတ်တော်ချုပ်)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

apex_courts.name: Constitutional Tribunal of the Union
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Tribunal with original jurisdiction over constitutional review.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Health, Myanmar
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator of health and allied health professionals including clinical psychology.

anonymisation_convention: Myanmar family-court decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Guardians and Wards Act
key_developments.description: Colonial-era guardianship statute applying across religious communities residually.

key_developments.title: Burma Muslim Dissolution of Marriages Act
Burma Buddhist Women's Special Marriage Act
key_developments.description: Federal Burmese Buddhist marriage statute enacted.

key_developments.title: Child Rights Law 2019
key_developments.description: Federal children's-rights statute aligned with UNCRC, replacing 1993 Child Law.

structural_findings: Myanmar operates a structurally distinctive multi-religious-personal-law framework — Buddhist (Dhammathat + 1954 Act), Hindu, Christian, and Muslim (1953 Act) personal laws operating in parallel alongside colonial-era Guardians and Wards Act 1890.
Customary Dhammathat tradition is structurally distinctive — only jurisdiction in corpus with Theravada-Buddhist customary-law personal-status framework.
Non-Hague Convention status places Myanmar in the non-Hague Southeast Asian cluster.
Psychology profession regulation operates through Ministry of Health framework — lacks unified federal-statutory psychology regulator.
editorial_notes: Myanmar jurisdiction sidecar — mixed-legal-system framework (English common-law substantive + Buddhist/Hindu/Christian/Muslim personal laws). Buddhist Women's Special Marriage Act 1954 + Muslim Dissolution Act 1953 + Guardians and Wards Act 1890 + Child Rights Law 2019 + non-Hague Convention.
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator.
Joins Southeast Asian + Theravada-Buddhist-customary-personal-law distinctive cluster + non-Hague Convention clusters within the corpus.
